storage_extensions.usage: mmap both files. Pass 1: parallel sequential scan with linear run-length aggregation (no hash map, no scatter). Pass 2: use recorded (start, end) positions from Pass 1 to sum l_quantity for each qualifying orderkey without re-scanning all 60M rows.

join_order.notes: Pass 2 iterates over 624 qualifying keys directly — no lineitem scan needed

filters.notes: Applied after accumulating the full run-sum for each orderkey in Pass 1

scan.access_pattern: sequential (single pass in Pass 1 only; Pass 2 uses recorded positions)

data_structures.pass1_thread_local: struct BoundaryState { int32_t first_key, last_key; double first_partial_sum, last_partial_sum; int64_t first_end_pos, last_start_pos; } per thread — tiny, L1-resident
data_structures.pass1_qualifying_keys: std::vector<QualifyingKey> per thread, where QualifyingKey = { int32_t key; double sum_qty; int64_t start_pos; int64_t end_pos; } — holds interior (complete) qualifying keys found by this thread
data_structures.pass2_groups: std::vector<GroupEntry> of ~624 entries — { int32_t orderkey; double sum_qty; int32_t orders_row; int32_t cust_row; }
data_structures.output_buffer: std::vector<Result> (~624 entries), sorted and top-100 emitted

passes.name: pass1_parallel_sorted_scan_aggregate
passes.description: Parallel morsel-driven scan of sorted_l_orderkey + sorted_l_quantity with linear run-length aggregation. No hash map, no scatter buffers. Each thread processes a contiguous range [row0, row1) of the sorted arrays. Within each chunk: maintain running (current_key, running_sum, run_start_pos). When key changes: if running_sum > 300, emit QualifyingKey{key, sum, run_start, run_end} to thread-local qualifying_keys vector. Record boundary state for first and last key in each thread chunk (these may be split across thread boundaries).

boundary_handling.first_key_in_chunk: Record first_key = sorted_l_orderkey[row0], accumulate partial sum from row0 until key changes. Store as (first_key, first_partial_sum, first_end_pos) in thread boundary struct.
boundary_handling.last_key_in_chunk: Record last_key = sorted_l_orderkey[row1-1], accumulate partial sum backwards from row1-1 until key changes (or beginning of the last run). Store as (last_key, last_partial_sum, last_start_pos). If first_key == last_key (entire chunk is same key), collapse into single boundary entry.
boundary_handling.interior_keys: All keys with runs fully contained within [row0, row1) are complete and can be filtered/emitted directly.

passes.name: pass1_boundary_merge
passes.description: Serial post-pass: after all threads complete, merge adjacent thread boundary states. For each pair of adjacent threads t and t+1: if thread[t].last_key == thread[t+1].first_key, combine their partial sums. The combined sum determines whether this boundary-spanning key qualifies. Collect all qualifying keys (from interior vectors + boundary merge) into a single global qualifying_keys array, including their (start_pos, end_pos) in the sorted arrays.

passes.name: pass2_qualifying_key_lookups
passes.description: Serial pass over ~624 qualifying orderkeys. For each qualifying key: (1) use recorded (start_pos, end_pos) to directly sum sorted_l_quantity[start_pos..end_pos] — no binary search needed, positions already known from Pass 1; (2) probe o_orderkey_hash to get orders_row; (3) read o_custkey[orders_row], o_orderdate[orders_row], o_totalprice[orders_row]; (4) probe c_custkey_hash to get cust_row. Store GroupEntry in results vector. No lineitem scan — only 624 hash probes and ~624×4=2496 sequential quantity reads.
passes.eliminates: Full second 60M-row lineitem scan from iter_1 (was 25ms)

parallelism.notes: Pass 2 is serial because it only has ~624 qualifying keys — parallelism overhead would dominate

indexes.purpose: join_lookup: qualifying l_orderkey → orders row index

output.date_decode: convert o_orderdate (int32 days since 1970-01-01) to YYYY-MM-DD for output only (100 rows)

optimization_notes: Root cause (Category E — Storage Extension): The iter_1 scatter/gather Pass 1 moves 1.6GB of data (64 threads × 64 partitions × 32768 slots × 12B) through DRAM-speed random writes during scatter (Phase A) and DRAM-speed reads during gather (Phase B), far exceeding the 44MB LLC. Each of the 60M scatter writes lands at a random strided location in a 24MB-per-thread address range, making every write a DRAM miss. The fix builds a one-time storage extension: lineitem sorted by l_orderkey (two files: sorted_l_orderkey.bin 240MB + sorted_l_quantity.bin 480MB, built with 2-pass LSD radix sort in 4.3s). With sorted data, Pass 1 becomes a single sequential read of 720MB with linear run-length aggregation: each thread scans a contiguous chunk, maintains a running (key, sum) accumulator, and emits qualifying keys with their start/end positions when the key changes — zero hash operations, zero scatter, zero DRAM-random writes. A tiny serial boundary merge handles keys split across thread chunk boundaries. Pass 2 also changes fundamentally: instead of re-scanning all 60M lineitem rows, it iterates over only ~624 qualifying keys, using the (start_pos, end_pos) positions recorded in Pass 1 to directly read the quantity ranges (no binary search needed), then performs the hash probes and customer lookup. This eliminates the entire 60M-row Pass 2 scan. Expected Pass 1: ~10-20ms (720MB sequential / 64 threads). Expected Pass 2: ~1-2ms (624 direct lookups). Total expected: ~15-25ms.
